narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, you've put significant effort into your application, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 31. Unfortunately, the admissions model predicts an 82.3% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is the key reason your application was rejected—schools use this prediction to ensure they admit students likely to succeed in the profession. The model's decision was driven primarily by your academic rankings in school: you placed in the 2nd decile for both your Year 1 and Year 3 grades, far below the typical student's 6th decile or higher, and these were the strongest factors pushing the prediction toward failure. Your Year 3 ranking had the single biggest negative impact, signaling to the model a consistent struggle with coursework compared to most peers. Your Year 1 ranking reinforced this concern, as it also fell well below average. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA actually nudged the prediction slightly toward success, performing a bit better relative to typical applicants despite being under the 3.21 average. Your LSAT score, while below the usual 36.7, had a smaller pulling effect toward failure, and your family income and full-time status had minimal influence either way. Overall, your profile stands out from typical admitted students, who boast stronger decile rankings and higher test scores that align with bar exam passers. This isn't a judgment on your potential or character—many factors beyond these metrics shape a legal career. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your growth and resilience. You're capable of turning this around, and with targeted improvements in your standout weak areas, future models and admissions teams will see your true promise. Keep pushing—you've got this.
